aliases: Low Carb Beef Bowl with Kimchi

english: Low Carb Beef Bowl with Kimchi

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for gyudon or kimchi gyudon. FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for gyudon or kimchi gyudon specifically. A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest defensible parent. The dish also contains kimchi, tofu, and salad vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for kimchi gyudon or low-carbohydrate beef bowl.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising simmered beef slices with kimchi over a base of mixed salad greens and silken tofu, as served at Sukiya Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, short plate, outside skirt steak, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised (USDA SR Legacy 13360) combined with component estimation for kimchi (USDA FoodData Central 168589), firm tofu (USDA FoodData Central 172475), and mixed salad greens (USDA FoodData Central 169247); weighted composite per dish proportion
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct database entry exists for Kimchi Low Carb Gyudon. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef (braised thin slices ~55% by weight), kimchi (~15%), firm tofu (~20%), and mixed salad greens (~10%), reflecting the rice-free, low-carbohydrate preparation. Sauce contribution (soy sauce, mirin, dashi) estimated at minor fraction. Values are per 100 g of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; combined with University of Sydney GI Database entries for kimchi (GI ~15), tofu (GI ~15), and mixed salad greens (GI ~15); beef contributes negligible GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kimchi Low Carb Gyudon. The dish replaces rice with salad greens and tofu, dramatically reducing glycemic carbohydrate load. GI estimated at 32 based on weighted average of component GI values: cooked beef (GI ~0, no glycemic carbohydrate), kimchi (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database), firm tofu (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database), mixed salad greens (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database), and sauce components (soy sauce, mirin — minor contribution, estimated GI ~50 for mirin fraction). Weighted composite yields approximately GI 32. GL per serving = (4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 380 g serving / 100) × 32 / 100 = 5.1. Classified as low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 1596 mg sodium per standard 380 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that one serving provides approximately 1596 mg sodium, representing 69–80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion control or requesting reduced-sauce preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and low net carbohydrate content (~16 g per serving) due to rice substitution with salad greens and tofu
type2_diabetes.guidance: The rice-free preparation substantially reduces the glycemic load compared to standard gyudon; with an estimated GL of 5.1 per serving and low GI of approximately 32, this dish is a suitable lower-carbohydrate option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the sodium content warrants monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef (~2.3 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides moderate saturated fat and high sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose lean beef preparations, and consider the cumulative sodium intake from this meal within their daily dietary plan.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Low Carb Gyudon is a Japanese-origin beef bowl dish served at Sukiya Singapore in which the traditional steamed rice base is replaced with a combination of mixed salad greens and silken tofu, making it a significantly lower-carbohydrate alternative to standard gyudon. The dish features thinly sliced beef simmered in a dashi-based sauce seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, topped with fermented kimchi, and served over the salad and tofu base. The flavour profile combines the savoury umami of the simmered beef and dashi sauce with the spicy, tangy, and slightly sour notes of kimchi, balanced by the mild, cooling texture of the salad greens and the soft, neutral creaminess of tofu. The elimination of rice reduces the carbohydrate content to approximately 16 g per serving compared to approximately 60–70 g in a standard gyudon, while maintaining high protein content from both beef and tofu. This dish is particularly relevant for individuals following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns, those managing Type 2 Diabetes seeking lower glycemic load meals, and individuals monitoring caloric intake. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 32 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 5.1. Sodium content is elevated due to the soy-based sauce and kimchi, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish provides approximately 10.8 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a caloric density of approximately 118 kcal per 100 g.
common_user_queries: Beef bowl with tofu instead of rice

confidence_reason: No direct database entry exists for Kimchi Low Carb Gyudon. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients (cooked beef, kimchi, tofu, salad greens) in proportions consistent with the dish description. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for the individual components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the dish composition is well-defined from the restaurant description.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries for the component ingredients at the level of precision required for this composite estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are reported as best-estimate values rather than confirmed measurements. The dish is served at Sukiya Singapore (Funan outlet) as menu item 133. The cuisine tags in the input JSON included 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' which are clearly erroneous tags from the source platform and have been disregarded. The food_id GD000975 is preserved from the input. Price SGD 9.00 is noted from the field survey source. Sodium is elevated primarily from kimchi and soy-based sauce; individuals with hypertension should exercise caution. The low-carbohydrate designation is valid as rice is replaced by salad greens and tofu, reducing carbohydrate content substantially relative to standard gyudon.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed menu number prefix '133.' from food_name to produce canonical English name.
Added Japanese local name キムチ低糖質牛丼 and romanized form Kimchi Teitōshitsu Gyūdon to satisfy G6 for Japanese cuisine.
Constructed weighted composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central component entries for beef, kimchi, tofu, and salad greens.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg as -1 with explicit justification in notes field to satisfy G3.
Set GI to 32 (numeric, >0) with analogous estimate basis from University of Sydney GI Database component values.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in aliases.
Set all ontology match_type values to allowed values only: closest_parent or none.
Disregarded erroneous cuisine tags (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers) from input source.
